title.en: The Complete Screenshot to Flutter Guide

h1.en: Screenshot to Flutter: The Complete Guide

description.en: A complete guide to converting UI screenshots into Flutter Dart widget code using AI — covering widget tree structure, Material Design mapping, StatefulWidget patterns, and integration tips.

intro.en: Flutter's widget-based UI model makes it one of the most powerful targets for screenshot-to-code AI tools. Unlike HTML where one element can have many styling properties, Flutter's widget tree expresses every visual property as a composable widget or parameter. This guide covers how AI converts screenshots to Flutter, what the output looks like, and how to get the best results.

content.en: In Flutter, everything is a widget — layout, styling, interactivity, and content. A simple card with text and a button in HTML might be a <div> with a few CSS rules. In Flutter, it becomes a Card widget containing a Column widget containing a Text widget and an ElevatedButton widget, with Padding and SizedBox widgets managing spacing. This nested composition is Flutter's defining characteristic. AI screenshot-to-Flutter tools must understand this tree model deeply — recognizing that a horizontal list of items should become a Row, that a scrollable list should use ListView.builder, and that a layered background becomes a Stack.

Flutter's Material library provides a rich set of pre-built components that map directly to common UI patterns. When the AI identifies a navigation bar in your screenshot, it generates a BottomNavigationBar or NavigationBar (Material 3). A card becomes a Card with a shadow. A text input becomes a TextField wrapped in a TextFormField for validation support. Buttons map to specific widget types: ElevatedButton for primary actions, TextButton for secondary actions, OutlinedButton for bordered styles, and IconButton for icon-only actions. This precise mapping is what differentiates a good Flutter AI tool from one that just wraps everything in generic Container widgets.

Layout is where Flutter-specific AI knowledge matters most. Horizontal arrangements become Row; vertical arrangements become Column. Overlapping layers use Stack with Positioned children. For flexible space distribution, Expanded and Flexible wrap children inside Row or Column. Scrollable content uses SingleChildScrollView or ListView. Grid layouts use GridView or Wrap. The AI must correctly identify which layout pattern applies from the visual screenshot — a two-column card grid becomes GridView.count(crossAxisCount: 2), not two nested Rows. Getting this right is the difference between code that compiles immediately and code that requires major restructuring.

Flutter has two base widget types. StatelessWidget is for UI that doesn't change after being built — a static card, a header, an icon. StatefulWidget has mutable state that triggers UI rebuilds when changed — a counter, a toggle switch, a form. AI tools infer the correct type from the screenshot content: a toggle button clearly needs StatefulWidget; a purely informational card can be StatelessWidget. Modern Flutter development may also use Riverpod or BLoC for state management, but AI-generated code typically uses built-in setState() as the baseline, which you can migrate to your state management solution afterward.

The typical integration workflow: copy the generated .dart file into your lib/ directory, review the import statements at the top (the AI generates correct dart:material imports), and add the widget to your widget tree by instantiating it with MyGeneratedWidget(). If the AI generated any placeholder properties (like a required String title parameter), pass real values when using the widget. Review and replace any hardcoded color hex values with your app's ThemeData colors for consistent theming. Run flutter format on the file for correct Dart formatting, then hot reload to see the result instantly.

question.en: Does the generated Flutter code support both iOS and Android?

answer.en: Yes. Flutter's single codebase compiles to native iOS and Android. The generated widget code runs identically on both platforms by default. Material Design widgets render with a Material look on Android and adapt to iOS conventions when the Cupertino variant is used. For platform-specific UI, the AI adds Theme.of(context).platform checks where relevant.

question.en: What Flutter packages are used in the generated code?

answer.en: The base generated code uses only flutter/material.dart and flutter/cupertino.dart — the standard Flutter SDK. No additional pub.dev packages are imported by default. If your screenshot shows patterns that match popular packages (like a Lottie animation, a charts widget, or a Google Map), the AI notes these as comments with suggested packages to add, but generates placeholder containers in the base output.

question.en: Can I use the generated code with Provider or Riverpod?

answer.en: Yes. The generated StatefulWidget components use setState() for local state. Migrating to Riverpod means replacing setState() with ref.watch() and moving state logic into a StateNotifier or Provider. The widget structure and UI code stays the same — only the state management layer changes. The generated code is designed as a clean UI layer that is easy to connect to any state management solution.

question.en: What resolution screenshot gives the best Flutter output?

answer.en: Screenshots at 390×844px (iPhone 14 size) or 360×800px (common Android size) work well for mobile UI screenshots. For tablet or desktop Flutter layouts, use 768px or wider. Higher resolution screenshots (2x or @2x exported from design tools) give the AI more detail to work with, improving color accuracy and spacing precision. Avoid overly compressed JPEG screenshots — use PNG for crisp text and sharp edges.
